splits.legalQuestion: Whether a multiemployer pension plan actuary may select actuarial assumptions for calculating withdrawal liability after the statutory measurement date (the end of the prior plan year), or whether all such assumptions must be fixed by that date.
splits.description: Circuits disagree on whether ERISA requires actuarial assumptions used to calculate employer withdrawal liability from multiemployer pension plans to be locked in by the end of the prior plan year, or whether actuaries may select assumptions after that date. The outcome affects billions of dollars in potential obligations for thousands of employers participating in multiemployer plans.

positions.label: Assumptions must be fixed by measurement date
positions.summary: Holds that all actuarial assumptions for calculating withdrawal liability must be selected by the statutory measurement date (end of the prior plan year), providing employers greater predictability.

positions.label: Assumptions may be selected after measurement date
positions.summary: Holds that the plan actuary may select actuarial assumptions after the statutory measurement date, allowing use of more current data in calculating withdrawal liability.

splits.relatedScotusTitle: M & K Employee Solutions v. Trustees of the IAM National Pension Fund

splits.legalQuestion: Whether the term of supervised release is tolled (paused) when a supervisee absconds from supervision, thereby extending the period during which the person can be found to have violated the conditions of supervised release.
splits.description: Circuits are split on whether federal courts may apply a judicially-created 'fugitive tolling' doctrine to supervised release, effectively extending the period of court-ordered supervision beyond the time specified in the judgment when a person absconds. This affects sentencing calculations at revocation hearings for thousands of individuals who abscond from supervised release each year and raises questions about the limits of judicial power to extend punishment without explicit statutory authorization.

positions.label: Tolling applies
positions.summary: Holds that the term of supervised release is tolled during the period a supervisee absconds, so the supervised release period does not run while the person is a fugitive.

positions.label: Tolling does not apply
positions.summary: Holds that supervised release is not tolled during absconding because there is no statutory basis for a judicially-created fugitive tolling doctrine in the supervised release context.

splits.legalQuestion: Whether Section 2244(b)(1)'s bar on relitigating previously raised claims in habeas proceedings applies to federal prisoners filing motions under Section 2255.
splits.description: Circuits are split 6-to-3 on whether federal prisoners face a categorical bar under § 2244(b)(1) from raising any claim they previously presented in a successive § 2255 motion. If (b)(1) applies, federal prisoners face a stricter gatekeeping requirement than the separate requirements of § 2255(h); if not, they need only satisfy § 2255(h)'s requirements of newly discovered evidence or a new rule of constitutional law.

positions.label: Section 2244(b)(1) applies to § 2255 motions
positions.summary: Holds that § 2244(b)(1)'s bar on relitigating previously raised claims applies to federal prisoners filing successive § 2255 motions, categorically barring them from re-raising claims.

positions.label: Section 2244(b)(1) does not apply to § 2255 motions
positions.summary: Holds that § 2244(b)(1) does not apply to federal prisoners' successive § 2255 motions, so they need only satisfy § 2255(h)'s own gatekeeping requirements.

splits.legalQuestion: Whether a candidate for federal office has Article III standing to challenge a state law that allows the counting of mail-in ballots received after Election Day.
splits.description: Circuits disagree on whether candidates have standing to bring pre-election challenges to voting procedures. If standing is broadly granted, it would make it far easier to challenge election administration practices nationwide before elections occur. If denied, such disputes would be channeled into post-election litigation with different practical dynamics.

positions.label: Candidate has standing
positions.summary: Holds that a candidate has Article III standing to challenge state election rules pre-election based on competitive injury from allegedly unlawful ballot-counting procedures.

positions.label: Candidate lacks standing
positions.summary: Holds that a candidate's alleged injury from a state law permitting late-counted ballots is too speculative to confer Article III standing before the election occurs.

splits.relatedScotusTitle: Bost v. Illinois State Board of Elections

splits.legalQuestion: Whether the rule of lenity takes precedence over agency or Sentencing Commission deference when interpreting ambiguous criminal sentencing provisions.
splits.description: Circuits are split on whether lenity trumps deference to agency interpretations in the criminal context. At least three circuits hold that lenity prevails over deference, while the Third Circuit has held that agency deference trumps lenity. The issue has become more salient after Loper Bright overruled Chevron and the Supreme Court cast doubt on deferring to agencies in criminal contexts.

positions.label: Lenity trumps deference
positions.summary: Holds that the rule of lenity takes precedence over Auer/agency deference when interpreting ambiguous criminal or sentencing provisions, preventing agencies from expanding criminal liability through interpretive guidance.

positions.label: Deference trumps lenity
positions.summary: Holds that agency or Sentencing Commission deference takes precedence over the rule of lenity when interpreting ambiguous Guidelines enhancements or criminal provisions, allowing anti-defendant readings based on agency expertise.

splits.legalQuestion: Whether the word 'and' in the First Step Act's safety-valve provision, 18 U.S.C. § 3553(f)(1), is conjunctive (requiring all three criminal-history criteria to disqualify a defendant) or disjunctive (any one criterion is disqualifying).
splits.description: Circuits are deeply split on whether 'and' means 'and' or 'or' in the safety-valve provision that allows defendants to be sentenced without regard to statutory minimums. The Ninth and Eleventh Circuits read it conjunctively (all three criteria must be met to disqualify), while the Fifth, Sixth, Seventh, and Eighth Circuits read it disjunctively (any single criterion disqualifies). This leads to dramatically different sentencing outcomes for identically-situated defendants depending on geographic location.

positions.label: 'And' is conjunctive — all three criteria required
positions.summary: Holds that defendants are disqualified from safety-valve relief only if they meet all three criminal-history criteria listed in § 3553(f)(1), because 'and' unambiguously means 'and.'

positions.label: 'And' is disjunctive — any one criterion disqualifies
positions.summary: Holds that possessing any one of the three criminal-history criteria in § 3553(f)(1) disqualifies a defendant from safety-valve relief, reading 'and' as distributive or disjunctive in context.

splits.legalQuestion: Whether Application Note 1 to U.S.S.G. § 4B1.2, which includes inchoate offenses like conspiracy within the definition of 'controlled substance offense,' is entitled to deference when the guideline text does not mention such offenses.
splits.description: Circuits disagree on whether the Sentencing Commission's commentary expanding 'controlled substance offense' to include conspiracy and other inchoate offenses can override the plain text of § 4B1.2(b), which does not mention inchoate offenses. Some circuits defer to the commentary under Stinson, while others hold the guideline text is unambiguous and the commentary exceeds the Commission's interpretive authority, especially after Kisor v. Wilkie narrowed deference principles.

positions.label: Commentary is entitled to deference
positions.summary: Holds that Application Note 1 is a valid, authoritative interpretation of an ambiguous guideline and that conspiracy qualifies as a 'controlled substance offense' for career-offender purposes.

positions.label: Commentary exceeds guideline text
positions.summary: Holds that the guideline text unambiguously excludes inchoate offenses and the commentary impermissibly expands the definition, so conspiracy does not qualify as a 'controlled substance offense.'

splits.legalQuestion: Whether non-retroactive changes in law, such as the First Step Act's anti-stacking amendment to 18 U.S.C. § 924(c), may be considered as an extraordinary and compelling reason for compassionate release under 18 U.S.C. § 3582(c)(1)(A).
splits.description: Circuits are split on whether district courts may consider non-retroactive changes in sentencing law—particularly the First Step Act's elimination of mandatory consecutive 'stacked' § 924(c) sentences—as a basis for compassionate release. Some circuits bar consideration entirely, while others permit it as one factor among many. The Sentencing Commission's 2024 policy statement amendment (§ 1B1.13(b)(6)) attempted to authorize such consideration, but some circuits have held the Commission exceeded its authority.

positions.label: Non-retroactive changes cannot support compassionate release
positions.summary: Holds that a non-retroactive change in sentencing law cannot contribute to a finding of extraordinary and compelling reasons for compassionate release, whether alone or combined with other factors.

positions.label: Non-retroactive changes may be considered
positions.summary: Holds that district courts may consider non-retroactive changes in sentencing law, including the First Step Act's anti-stacking amendment, as one factor in evaluating whether extraordinary and compelling reasons exist for compassionate release.

splits.legalQuestion: Whether the Guidelines' definition of 'controlled substance offense' in U.S.S.G. § 4B1.2(b) encompasses state drug convictions involving substances not controlled under the federal Controlled Substances Act.
splits.description: Circuits disagree on whether a state drug conviction qualifies as a 'controlled substance offense' under the career offender or other Guidelines provisions when the state controls a substance not on federal schedules. Some circuits hold the text broadly covers any state-law offense, while others require the substance to be federally controlled. The Supreme Court addressed a related question in Brown v. United States (2024) but the split persists on the Guidelines issue.

positions.label: State-only controlled substances qualify
positions.summary: Holds that the Guidelines' definition of 'controlled substance offense' includes state drug offenses regardless of whether the substance is controlled under federal law, because the text refers broadly to 'an offense under state law.'

positions.label: Substance must be federally controlled
positions.summary: Holds that a 'controlled substance offense' under the Guidelines requires the substance at issue to be controlled under federal law, grafting a federal-law limitation onto the guideline definition.

splits.legalQuestion: Whether federal prisoners challenging conditions of confinement or BOP decisions affecting the duration/terms of custody must proceed exclusively through habeas corpus, or whether civil rights actions (e.g., Bivens or the APA) are also available.
splits.description: A longstanding circuit split exists on whether habeas corpus is the exclusive remedy for federal prisoners challenging BOP decisions that affect the execution of their sentences (such as First Step Act time credit calculations), or whether statutory or constitutional civil rights claims may also be brought. This implicates procedural requirements like exhaustion, filing fees, and available remedies. The Supreme Court has noted but not resolved this question since Preiser v. Rodriguez (1973).

positions.label: Habeas is the exclusive remedy
positions.summary: Holds that challenges to the fact or duration of confinement, including BOP decisions about time credits and prerelease custody, must be brought exclusively through habeas corpus.

positions.label: Civil rights or statutory claims also available
positions.summary: Holds that prisoners may bring non-habeas civil claims (e.g., APA or Bivens) to challenge BOP decisions affecting confinement, at least when the claim does not directly seek release.

splits.legalQuestion: Whether the civil or criminal appeal deadline applies to an order resolving a Hyde Amendment motion for attorney's fees filed in a criminal case.
splits.description: Circuits disagree on whether the 30-day civil deadline or the 14-day criminal deadline applies to appeals from orders denying Hyde Amendment attorney's fee motions. The majority view treats the motion as civil because it is ancillary to the underlying criminal case, while a minority applies the criminal deadline. The distinction can be dispositive of appellate jurisdiction.

positions.label: Civil deadline applies
positions.summary: Holds that a Hyde Amendment motion for attorney's fees is a civil matter ancillary to the criminal case, so the 30-day civil appeal deadline under Fed. R. App. P. 4(a) governs.

positions.label: Criminal deadline applies
positions.summary: Holds that because the Hyde Amendment motion arises within and is part of the criminal case, the 14-day criminal appeal deadline under Fed. R. App. P. 4(b) applies.

splits.legalQuestion: Whether post-petition, pre-conversion increases in property equity belong to the bankruptcy estate or to debtors who convert in good faith from Chapter 13 to Chapter 7 under 11 U.S.C. § 348(f)(1)(A).
splits.description: Circuits disagree on whether appreciation in the value of estate property between the filing of a Chapter 13 petition and conversion to Chapter 7 becomes part of the converted Chapter 7 estate. The Ninth Circuit holds that appreciation inures to the estate based on the broad scope of § 541(a), while other circuits hold that § 348(f)(1)(A) protects good-faith converting debtors by limiting the converted estate to asset values as of the original petition date.

positions.label: Appreciation belongs to the estate
positions.summary: Holds that post-petition, pre-conversion increases in property equity are part of the converted Chapter 7 estate because § 541(a) broadly defines estate property to include all interests as of filing, including subsequent appreciation.

positions.label: Appreciation belongs to the debtor
positions.summary: Holds that § 348(f)(1)(A) limits the converted estate to property values as of the original filing date, so post-petition, pre-conversion appreciation belongs to the good-faith converting debtor, not the estate.

splits.legalQuestion: Whether a district court may expressly punish a defendant for violations of supervised release at revocation, or whether the purpose of revocation sentencing is limited to sanctioning the breach of trust.
splits.description: Circuits disagree on the permissible purposes of supervised release revocation sentences. The Sixth Circuit permits courts expressly to punish defendants for supervised release violations, while other circuits hold that revocation sentencing must focus on the breach of the court's trust rather than punishment for the underlying violation conduct. The Sixth Circuit's position has been criticized as placing it at the extreme of a circuit split.

positions.label: Punishment for violations is permissible
positions.summary: Holds that district courts may expressly punish defendants for their supervised release violations at revocation sentencing, not just for the breach of the court's trust.

positions.label: Revocation limited to breach of trust
positions.summary: Holds that the purpose of supervised release revocation is to sanction the breach of the court's trust, not to punish the defendant for the underlying violative conduct.

splits.legalQuestion: Whether an order denying a motion to seal court documents containing private medical information is immediately appealable under the collateral order doctrine.
splits.description: The D.C. Circuit held that maintaining privacy in medical treatments and diagnoses is a value of 'high order' warranting immediate appellate review of orders denying motions to seal, while other circuits have been more restrictive in applying the collateral order doctrine to sealing disputes. The question impacts whether litigants must wait until final judgment to challenge public disclosure of sensitive medical information.

positions.label: Immediately appealable
positions.summary: Holds that orders denying motions to seal medical information are immediately appealable under the collateral order doctrine because medical privacy is a value of high order that would be irreparably lost without immediate review.

positions.label: Not immediately appealable
positions.summary: Holds that orders denying motions to seal are not immediately appealable under the collateral order doctrine because litigants can seek post-judgment relief or other remedies.

splits.legalQuestion: Whether the Religious Land Use and Institutionalized Persons Act (RLUIPA) authorizes individual-capacity damages suits against state prison officials.
splits.description: Circuits are split on whether RLUIPA creates a cause of action for money damages against state prison officials in their individual capacities. Some circuits have held that RLUIPA, enacted under the Spending Clause, does not clearly authorize personal-liability damages against individuals who are not direct recipients of federal funds, while others have allowed such claims to proceed.

positions.label: RLUIPA authorizes individual-capacity damages
positions.summary: Holds that RLUIPA provides a cause of action for money damages against state prison officials sued in their individual capacities for violations of prisoners' religious exercise rights.

positions.label: RLUIPA does not authorize individual-capacity damages
positions.summary: Holds that RLUIPA does not clearly authorize individual-capacity damages suits against state prison officials because Congress cannot impose personal liability on non-recipient individuals through the Spending Clause.

circuits.name: Fifth Circuit
circuits.caseName: Sossamon v. Lone Star State of Texas

splits.relatedScotusTitle: Landor v. Louisiana Department of Corrections and Public Safety

splits.legalQuestion: Whether the Federal Aviation Administration Authorization Act of 1994 (FAAAA) preempts state-law negligence claims against freight brokers who arrange transportation of property by motor carriers.
splits.description: Circuits are split on whether the FAAAA's broad preemption provision, which bars states from enacting or enforcing laws 'related to' a broker's services, preempts state tort negligence claims against freight brokers for injuries arising from the transportation they arranged. The split affects the ability of accident victims to hold brokers accountable for negligent carrier selection.

positions.label: FAAAA preempts negligence claims
positions.summary: Holds that the FAAAA's preemption clause bars state-law negligence claims against freight brokers because such claims are 'related to' the broker's services and would effectively regulate broker operations.

circuits.name: Ninth Circuit
circuits.caseName: Miller v. C.H. Robinson Worldwide, Inc.

positions.label: FAAAA does not preempt negligence claims
positions.summary: Holds that state-law negligence claims against freight brokers are not preempted because they are generally applicable tort standards that do not specifically target broker services.

splits.relatedScotusTitle: Montgomery v. Caribe Transport II, LLC

splits.legalQuestion: Whether a district court may consider factors related to potential errors or unfairness in a defendant's conviction or sentence as part of the 'extraordinary and compelling reasons' analysis for compassionate release under 18 U.S.C. § 3582(c)(1)(A).
splits.description: The circuits disagree on whether claims that could also be raised in a habeas petition under § 2255 — such as allegations of legal error in the underlying conviction — may be considered as part of the compassionate-release analysis. Some circuits allow consideration of such factors while others prohibit it, viewing compassionate release as an improper substitute for habeas.

positions.label: May consider conviction-related errors
positions.summary: Holds that district courts may consider factors related to potential errors in a defendant's conviction or sentence as one component of the extraordinary-and-compelling-reasons analysis under § 3582(c)(1)(A).

positions.label: May not consider conviction-related errors
positions.summary: Holds that compassionate release may not be used as a vehicle to challenge the underlying conviction or sentence, and such claims must be raised through habeas corpus under § 2255.

splits.legalQuestion: Whether the federal law prohibiting unlawful users of controlled substances from possessing firearms, 18 U.S.C. § 922(g)(3), violates the Second Amendment as applied to marijuana users.
splits.description: Following the Supreme Court's decision in Bruen establishing a historical-tradition test for Second Amendment challenges, the circuits have split on whether § 922(g)(3)'s prohibition on firearm possession by users of controlled substances (particularly marijuana) is constitutional. This implicates the intersection of expanding state marijuana legalization with federal firearms law.

positions.label: Section 922(g)(3) is constitutional
positions.summary: Holds that § 922(g)(3) does not violate the Second Amendment because historical tradition supports disarming individuals whose drug use renders them dangerous or untrustworthy with firearms.

positions.label: Section 922(g)(3) is unconstitutional as applied
positions.summary: Holds that § 922(g)(3) violates the Second Amendment as applied to marijuana users because the government has not demonstrated a sufficient historical tradition of disarming persons for drug use alone.

splits.legalQuestion: Whether workers who distribute baked goods to retail stores qualify as 'transportation workers engaged in foreign or interstate commerce' under Section 1 of the Federal Arbitration Act, thereby exempting them from mandatory arbitration agreements.
splits.description: Circuits disagree on how broadly to read the Section 1 exemption for transportation workers, particularly for last-mile delivery workers who transport goods within a single state as part of an interstate chain of commerce. This affects whether such workers can be compelled to arbitrate employment disputes.

positions.label: Broad reading — last-mile workers exempt
positions.summary: Holds that workers who are the last link in an interstate chain of commerce, even if operating within a single state, qualify as transportation workers engaged in interstate commerce and are exempt from the FAA.

circuits.name: First Circuit
circuits.caseName: Fraga v. Premium Retail Services, Inc.

positions.label: Narrow reading — last-mile workers not exempt
positions.summary: Holds that workers who deliver goods locally within a state do not qualify as transportation workers engaged in interstate commerce merely because the goods previously moved in interstate commerce.

splits.legalQuestion: Whether Section 47(b) of the Investment Company Act creates a private right of action allowing parties to a contract that violates the ICA to sue in federal court for rescission of that contract.
splits.description: Circuits disagree on whether § 47(b) of the ICA provides an implied private right of action for rescission of contracts made in violation of the Act. This affects the ability of private parties to enforce the ICA's protections against abusive investment company practices through direct federal court actions.

positions.label: Section 47(b) creates a private right of action
positions.summary: Holds that § 47(b) creates an implied private right of action for rescission of contracts that violate the Investment Company Act, allowing private parties to sue directly.

circuits.name: Second Circuit
circuits.caseName: FS Credit Opportunities Corp. v. Saba Capital Master Fund

positions.label: Section 47(b) does not create a private right of action
positions.summary: Holds that § 47(b) is a voidability provision rather than a private right of action, and only the SEC can enforce the ICA's prohibitions.

splits.relatedScotusTitle: FS Credit Opportunities Corp. v. Saba Capital Master Fund

splits.legalQuestion: Whether the Federal Insecticide, Fungicide, and Rodenticide Act (FIFRA) preempts state-law failure-to-warn claims based on allegedly inadequate product labeling when the EPA has not required the specific warning.
splits.description: Circuits are split on whether FIFRA's labeling requirements preempt state tort claims alleging that a pesticide manufacturer should have included additional warnings on its label. This affects products liability litigation involving herbicides and pesticides nationwide, including high-profile Roundup litigation.

positions.label: FIFRA preempts state failure-to-warn claims
positions.summary: Holds that FIFRA preempts state-law failure-to-warn claims because allowing such claims would impose labeling requirements 'in addition to or different from' those required under FIFRA.

positions.label: FIFRA does not preempt state failure-to-warn claims
positions.summary: Holds that state failure-to-warn claims are not preempted by FIFRA because they parallel, rather than impose requirements 'in addition to or different from,' federal labeling requirements.

splits.relatedScotusTitle: Monsanto Company v. John L. Durnell

splits.legalQuestion: Whether Section 2 of the Voting Rights Act, as construed under the Gingles framework, can constitutionally compel race-based redistricting as a remedy for vote dilution, and whether compliance with Section 2 constitutes a compelling governmental interest justifying the use of race in drawing district lines.
splits.description: This case raises the question of whether race-conscious redistricting mandated by Section 2 of the Voting Rights Act is constitutionally permissible under the Equal Protection Clause. Lower courts have applied the Gingles framework to require consideration of race, but the constitutionality of this framework as applied is increasingly contested.

positions.label: Section 2 compels constitutionally permissible race-conscious redistricting
positions.summary: Holds that compliance with Section 2 of the VRA, as interpreted under the Gingles framework, constitutes a compelling interest that justifies the use of race in drawing district lines.

positions.label: Section 2's compelled race-based redistricting raises constitutional concerns
positions.summary: Holds or suggests that the Gingles framework's requirement of race-based redistricting may exceed constitutional limits under the Equal Protection Clause, particularly after Dobbs-era equal protection jurisprudence.

circuits.name: Eighth Circuit
circuits.caseName: Arkansas State Conference of the NAACP v. Arkansas Board of Apportionment

splits.legalQuestion: What legal standards govern claims of race discrimination under 42 U.S.C. § 1981 in the operation of hospitality or business establishments, particularly regarding the scope of the right to make and enforce contracts?
splits.description: Circuits have taken different approaches to the standard for § 1981 claims in hospitality and service contexts, particularly regarding what constitutes interference with the right to make and enforce contracts and the available remedies. The Supreme Court granted certiorari to resolve the Second Circuit's approach against other circuits' frameworks.

positions.label: Narrower standard for § 1981 claims
positions.summary: Holds that § 1981 claims require a showing of interference with an actual contractual relationship or attempted contract, applying a more restrictive view of what constitutes actionable discrimination.

positions.label: Broader standard for § 1981 claims
positions.summary: Holds that § 1981 covers a broader range of discriminatory conduct in business establishments, including degraded service or hostile treatment that impairs the enjoyment of contracted services.
